case_name: Lithium reduction from mineralized well water for municipal drinking water supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for lithium-containing well water intended for drinking water use under moderate cost and stable-operation constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small municipality relies on deep well water with naturally elevated lithium associated with a mineralized groundwater aquifer. The utility needs a dependable treatment approach for drinking water production and prefers proven, steady systems over highly specialized or unstable processes. The raw water has moderate salinity and hardness, so matrix effects on selectivity and scaling need to be considered.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve substantial lithium reduction for drinking water protection while maintaining reliable compliance-quality production and acceptable finished water stability.
secondary_goals: use established and well-supported treatment technology
avoid unnecessarily high energy demand where practical

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: brine or concentrate handling is possible but should be limited and straightforward
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable continuous operation with predictable performance is required

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_for_scaling_control: reduce hardness or adjust chemistry to limit membrane scaling and maintain steady operation
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reject dissolved lithium along with other dissolved salts in mineralized groundwater
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for corrosion control and finished water stability

applicability_rationale: lithium is a dissolved monovalent ion and established selective low-complexity conventional removal options are limited for municipal drinking water service
reverse osmosis is a well-proven drinking water technology for dissolved inorganic contaminant reduction and provides a clear, supportable route for lithium reduction
the raw water has moderate TDS and hardness, making pretreatment important but manageable within standard membrane practice
the utility requires stable operation, and RO systems are widely implemented with predictable control strategies when scaling is managed
potential_risks: lithium rejection may be lower than divalent ions, so pilot testing and recovery optimization are important
hardness, alkalinity, and silica can cause scaling without adequate pretreatment
concentrate management remains a significant implementation issue

core_unit_functions.softening_or_antiscalant_pretreatment: limit hardness-related scaling and protect downstream membranes
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: remove a portion of dissolved salts and potentially reduce lithium, though generally less strongly than RO

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration is an established membrane option for brackish well water treatment and may fit utilities seeking somewhat lower pressure than RO
it can reduce dissolved constituents and may offer partial lithium control in some waters
this is more attractive when simultaneous hardness and salinity reduction are desired
potential_risks: lithium rejection can be insufficient because monovalent ion removal is often weaker with NF than with RO
finished water compliance for lithium may be less reliable across changing feed chemistry

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_filtration: remove particulates and protect the selective media bed
core_unit_functions.lithium_selective_ion_exchange_or_sorben: target lithium uptake using specialty media where applicable

applicability_rationale: specialty sorbents or ion-exchange materials may offer direct lithium targeting where source water chemistry is favorable
this approach can avoid high-pressure membrane operation if a suitable commercial media is demonstrated
it may be attractive for moderate flow systems if pilot data confirm capacity and selectivity
potential_risks: technology maturity for municipal drinking water lithium removal is less established than RO
high sodium and other competing ions in this water can reduce selectivity or bed life
media replacement or regenerant handling may create uncertain operating costs

core_unit_functions.lime_softening: reduce hardness and some scaling tendency before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide the main dissolved lithium removal barrier
core_unit_functions.stabilization: condition finished water for distribution stability

applicability_rationale: this train can improve membrane robustness where hardness control is a primary concern
it is technically sound for mineralized well water requiring dissolved contaminant removal
the combination may be considered if upstream softening is already practiced or scaling risk is especially problematic
potential_risks: more unit processes increase operator burden compared with direct RO pretreatment
softening sludge handling adds residuals complexity
this may be more treatment than needed for a straightforward lithium-focused case

core_unit_functions.electrodialysis_or_electrodialysis_rever: separate dissolved ions using electrically driven ion transport
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust product water chemistry for distribution

applicability_rationale: electrically driven desalting can treat brackish groundwater and may reduce lithium as part of broader ion removal
it can be considered as an alternative to pressure-driven membranes in some salinity ranges
potential_risks: lithium-specific performance is less commonly documented for standard municipal drinking water application than RO
system complexity and control demands may exceed the utility preference for straightforward stable operation

preferred_rank_pattern: reverse_osmosis_with_scaling_control should rank first for lithium reduction in mineralized well water
nanofiltration may appear as a secondary option but should rank below reverse osmosis due to less reliable monovalent ion rejection
specialty lithium-selective media may be considered but should not outrank established membrane treatment under stable-operation requirements

must_reflect: moderate cost and moderate energy sensitivity do not favor unnecessarily complex multi-stage treatment unless clearly justified
stable operation requirement should favor proven municipal desalting approaches over less mature lithium-specific options
residuals and concentrate management should be acknowledged but kept within manageable bounds

must_contain: why lithium behaves as a dissolved monovalent ion that is difficult to remove by ordinary conventional treatment
why reverse osmosis is the most established option among practical drinking water technologies for this case
why matrix effects such as hardness, TDS, sodium, and silica matter for treatment selection and pretreatment

should_mention: uncertainty in lithium rejection relative to other ions
membrane scaling and fouling risk from hard mineralized groundwater
performance uncertainty for selective sorbents under competing-ion conditions
